content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/ Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660           322-5901           322-6441           322-6444

May 5, 1978

Charles Kinney, Counselor
Associated Building Industry
of Northern California
3496 Breakwater Court
Hayward, CA  94545

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 77-025

Dear Mr. Kinney:

     At its May 2, 1978, meeting, the Fair Political
Practices Commission decided to dismiss the opinion
request you submitted since it can be answered without
reaching the question of whether an industry newsletter
comes within the exemption granted in Government Code
Section 83300(b)1/ to newspapers or periodicals of general
circulation. We are providing you with the following
informal advice which conforms with the interim oral
advice given to you by Barbara Campbell, an attorney in
the Legal Division.

     The Associated Building Industry of Northern
California ("ABI"), a nonprofit trade association repre-
senting the residential construction industry and associated
concerns, publishes two newsletters. The newsletters,
the Progress and the Regional Regulator Review ("Review"),2/
are published on a biweekly and monthly basis respectively.
The Review has been published since April of 1976. The
Progress has been published since 1974. Progress has a
circulation of approximately 1,700 and the Review is
distributed to 3,900. Progress is sent to ABI members
and nonmember subscribers who constitute approximately
20 percent of the readership. The Review is sent also

_________________________
1/ All further references are to the Government
Code.

2/ The Review is published under a yearly grant
given by the Construction Industry Advancement Fund. ABI
administers that grant.

Charles Kinney, Counselor
May 5, 1978
Page Two


to ABI members and to anyone who contributes to the
Construction Industry Advancement Fund. Subscriptions
are available for these newsletters upon request by any
member of the public. The Progress is available for a
$10 subscription. The Review is available free of charge
for up to ten copies.

     These newsletters highlight, from the builder's
viewpoint, the actions of local, state and federal agencies
and courts that affect the residential construction
industry. ABI receives press releases and notices of
meetings which will be of interest to its membership.
Staff monitors the actions and meetings and reports on
them in the newsletters. The Progress has a Calendar of
Events in each issue informing the readership of forthcoming
meetings and hearings. The newsletters on occasion may
urge directly or indirectly administrative action by
suggesting the readers communicate on a particular issue
with agency officials.

     Based on these facts, you have asked if the
ABI newsletters qualify as periodicals of general circulation
to come within the exemption from the lobbying provisions
of the Political Reform Act granted in Section 86300(b).
Upon review of your request, we have concluded that the
costs incurred by ABI in connection with the newsletters
will not require the filing of reports under the lobbying
provisions of the Political Reform Act. Therefore, it
is not necessary to reach the question of whether the
newsletters qualify as a periodical of general circulation.

     As stated above, ABI sometimes urges the readers
of its newsletters to communicate with agency officials.
Payments incurred in connection with such communication
may be reportable as payments to influence legislative
or administrative action. Section 82045(e). If ABI
made payments to influence legislative or administrative
action totaling $250 or more in one month, ABI would be
required to file a report under Section 86108(b).

     However, with specific reference to payments
which urge others to communicate with officials, the
Commission has provided by regulation that such payments
will be reportable if:

          Payments [are] made ... for the principal
     purpose of soliciting or urging other persons

Charles Kinney, Counselor
May 5, 1978
Page Three


to enter into direct communication with elective
state officials, legislative officials or
agency officials for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence legislative or
administrative action....

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18621(a)(4) (emphasis added).

Since the newsletters are not for the principal purpose
of soliciting or urging others to enter into direct
communication, the payments for the entire newsletter
will not be considered payments to influence legislative
or administrative action.

However, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18621(a)(5)
provides that any payment or portion not described in
18621(a)(4) which would not have been incurred but for
soliciting or urging others to communicate will be reportable.
Therefore, payments for that portion of ABIT's newsletters
which urge others to communicate directly will be reportable.
However, it is unlikely that the payments attributable
to the occasional references in either newsletter which
urge others to communicate directly with legislative or
agency officials will ever total $250 or more in a calendar
month.

This conclusion is reinforced by the fact that of
the three agencies about which you specifically inquired, two
are local agencies. (Association of Bay Area Governments and
the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District.) Payments to
influence administrative action under Section 82045(e) are
reportable if the direct communication urged from others is
with a state agency official. Section 82004.

You might be interested to know that the Commission
is proposing a legislative change in Section 86108(b) to
raise the monthly threshold to $2,500 from $250 (S.B. 2077).
The Commission is also in the process of revising its regulations
concerning lobbying disclosure. One of the proposals under
consideration includes a newsletter exemption similar to that
provided in the campaign disclosure area in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18225(c)(4)(C). That exemption would exclude the
reporting of payments for regularly published newsletters
sent to members or requestors, unless the newsletter is expanded
in frequency of distribution, size, format or circulation.

Charles Kinney, Counselor
May 5, 1978
Page Four


     I hope this information is helpful to you.  If you
have any further questions, please contact Barbara Campbell,
an attorney in our Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:BC:plh

sections.question: Whether the ABI newsletters qualify as periodic publications of general circulation to come within the exemption from the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act granted in Government Code Section 86300(b), and whether costs incurred in connection with the newsletters will require filing of reports under the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: Upon review of the facts, the FPPC determined that the costs incurred in connection with the newsletters will not require filing of reports under the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act, making it unnecessary to reach the question of whether the newsletters qualify as periodic publications of general circulation. Payments for portions of the newsletters that urge readers to communicate directly with legislative or agency officials will be reportable as payments to influence legislative or administrative action under Sections 82045(e) and 86204. However, such payments are unlikely to total $250 or more in a calendar month, particularly given that two of the three agencies specifically inquired about are local agencies.
sections.question_synthetic: Do ABI's two newsletters (Progress and Review) qualify as periodic publications of general circulation under the Political Reform Act exemption, and must costs associated with their publication be reported as lobbying expenditures?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that newsletter publication costs do not trigger lobbying disclosure filing requirements, though any costs specifically attributable to portions urging direct communication with officials would be reportable as lobbying expenditures. However, such attributable costs are unlikely to exceed the $250 monthly threshold, particularly for local agency matters.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is partially obscured/degraded with some text illegible or cut off. Question and conclusion sections are identifiable but some details in middle sections are unclear. The core legal advice is extractable despite the document quality issues. Key points regarding reportability of newsletter-related payments are clearly stated in final sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the ABI newsletters qualify as periodic publications of general circulation to come within the exemption from the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act granted in Government Code Section 86300(b), and whether costs incurred in connection with the newsletters will require filing of reports under the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Upon review of the facts, the FPPC determined that the costs incurred in connection with the newsletters will not require filing of reports under the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act, making it unnecessary to reach the question of whether the newsletters qualify as periodic publications of general circulation. Payments for portions of the newsletters that urge readers to communicate directly with legislative or agency officials will be reportable as payments to influence legislative or administrative action under Sections 82045(e) and 86204. However, such payments are unlikely to total $250 or more in a calendar month, particularly given that two of the three agencies specifically inquired about are local agencies.

embedding.summary: The FPPC dismisses an opinion request regarding whether ABI's newsletters qualify as periodicals of general circulation, instead providing informal advice that publication costs do not require lobbying disclosure reporting, though portions urging direct communication with officials would be reportable if they exceed $250 monthly.
